items.title: KimJongRAT Continues to Evolve by Leveraging LOTS
items.description: In May 2026, security researchers observed an attack campaign distributing KimJongRAT through GitHub and other legitimate services. KimJongRAT, used by the North Korean APT group Kimsuky since 2013, combines information stealing and remote access capabilities. The infection chain begins with phishing emails containing shortened URLs redirecting to GitHub Releases hosting malicious ZIP files. Victi
items.ai_summary: The KimJongRAT malware, used by the North Korean APT group Kimsuky since 2013, has been observed in a recent attack campaign leveraging legitimate services such as GitHub to distribute the malware. The infection chain begins with phishing emails containing shortened URLs that redirect to GitHub Releases hosting malicious ZIP files, which is classified as T1566 (Phishing). Once infected, the malware combines information stealing and remote access capabilities, allowing the threat actors to encrypt data for impact, classified as T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact). To remediate this threat, defenders should implement email filters to block phishing emails and restrict access to GitHub Releases, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual network traffic or encrypted files, and have an incident response plan in place in case of an attack.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FIFA 2026 Security Alert: Cybercriminals Exploit Fan Excitement with Mass Phishing
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting anticipation for the FIFA World Cup 2026 through sophisticated phishing campaigns targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. Attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure resolving to specific IP addresses, designed to harvest credentials and payment information. The campaigns feature con
items.ai_summary: The FIFA 2026 Security Alert involves a sophisticated phishing campaign targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. The attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure to harvest credentials and payment information, exploiting the anticipation for the FIFA World Cup 2026. The campaigns feature convincing content, increasing the likelihood of successful phishing attempts. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users on phishing detection, and enforce multi-factor authentication for all sensitive accounts. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious domain registrations and IP addresses associated with the phishing infrastructure can help identify and block potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From poisoned search results to GPU mining: A cryptojacking campaign abusing ScreenConnect and Microsoft .NET utilities
items.description: Microsoft Defender Experts identified an active cryptojacking campaign leveraging AI-assisted delivery mechanisms alongside traditional SEO poisoning. Attackers create fake download sites impersonating trusted utilities like CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting users with high-performance GPUs. Victims download ZIP archives containing legitimate executables bundled with malicious DLL
items.ai_summary: The cryptojacking campaign exploits users by leveraging SEO poisoning to create fake download sites for trusted utilities such as CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting those with high-performance GPUs. The attackers bundle legitimate executables with malicious DLLs within ZIP archives, which, when downloaded and executed, lead to the compromise of the system. This campaign affects users who download and run these tainted utilities, resulting in their systems being hijacked for cryptocurrency mining. To remediate, defenders should implement strict download filters, ensuring that only trusted sources are used for downloading software, and regularly monitor system resource usage for signs of unauthorized mining activity. Additionally, keeping software up-to-date and using anti-virus solutions that detect and block malicious DLLs can help prevent such attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608.006| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1496| D[Resource Hijacking]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Millenium: A RAT Rewritten, A Threat Multiplied
items.description: Group-IB analyzes Millenium RAT version 4.*, a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, while continuing to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. The malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD. Active exploitation campaigns are condu
items.ai_summary: The Millenium RAT version 4.* is a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, allowing it to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. This malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD, and its active exploitation campaigns are currently being conducted. If exploited, this malware can lead to data encryption and potential ransomware attacks, affecting various organizations and individuals who have not taken proper security measures. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection systems, focusing on unusual Telegram API activity, and ensure that all remote access software is up-to-date and securely configured. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a Zero Trust security model to limit the spread of potential malware infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework “DCloud Uni-App” Powers a Global Scam Economy
items.description: A Chinese web-development framework called DCloud Uni-App has become the technical foundation for over 236,000 scam domains since 2022, powering fake cryptocurrency exchanges, pig-butchering operations, wallet drainers, gambling platforms, and brand-impersonation sites. The framework gained prominence after the 2024 RainbowEx cryptocurrency scam in Argentina, which defrauded residents of San Pedro
items.ai_summary: The DCloud Uni-App framework has been utilized by threat actors to create and manage over 236,000 scam domains globally, primarily targeting cryptocurrency exchanges and wallet users. This framework has been instrumental in facilitating pig-butchering operations, wallet drainers, and brand-impersonation sites, resulting in significant financial losses for victims. The threat actors leverage T1586.002 to compromise email accounts and T1134 for access token manipulation, allowing them to maintain control over the scam domains. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement robust email security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular password resets, and monitor for suspicious access token activity. Additionally, cryptocurrency exchange and wallet providers should enhance their security protocols, including implementing robust Know-Your-Customer (KYC) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) checks to prevent fraudulent activities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CL-STA-1062 Targets Southeast Asian Governments and Critical Infrastructure
items.description: Throughout 2025, Chinese-speaking threat actors tracked as CL-STA-1062 conducted extensive operations against government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia, specifically targeting state-owned enterprises in energy and government sectors. Active since March 2022, this cluster was previously identified as UAT-7237 in campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. The atta
items.ai_summary: The CL-STA-1062 threat actor group has been conducting targeted operations against Southeast Asian governments and critical infrastructure, particularly focusing on state-owned enterprises in the energy and government sectors. The group, active since March 2022, was previously known for campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. Although specific technical details such as CVE numbers are not available, the real-world impact involves potential data breaches and disruption of critical services, affecting government entities and citizens. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement robust network monitoring, utilize intrusion detection systems, and enforce strict access controls, including multi-factor authentication for all users. Additionally, ensuring all software and systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, such as updating to the latest versions of operating systems and applications, is crucial.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group has identified STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor continuously developed and deployed by Russia-linked Turla (FSB Center 16) since December 2022. The multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections and targets government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. STOCKSTAY shares significant code o
items.ai_summary: The STOCKSTAY malware, a .NET backdoor, has been identified by Google's Threat Intelligence Group as a tool used by the Russia-linked Turla group since December 2022. This multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections, targeting government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. The malware's use of T1219 (Remote Access Software) and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) tactics indicates a high level of sophistication, allowing for remote access and data encryption. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network monitoring to detect unusual WebSocket connections and ensure all remote access software is up-to-date and securely configured. Additionally, implementing a zero-trust architecture and regularly updating threat intelligence feeds can help prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1566| E[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Photo ZIP campaign targeting hospitality industry delivers Node.js implant for persistent access
items.description: Since April 2026, a sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign has targeted hospitality and hotel organizations across Europe and Asia. The operation uses photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images. When executed, these shortcuts initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell, Node.js-based implants, and dual registry persistence mechanisms. The t
items.ai_summary: The Photo ZIP campaign targets the hospitality industry by using photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images, which when executed, initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell and Node.js-based implants. The attack chain utilizes T1059.001 to execute PowerShell commands, T1027 to obfuscate files and information, and T1036 to masquerade as legitimate files. To remediate, defenders should block shortcut files (.lnk) from being executed, update PowerShell to the latest version, and implement a Node.js security framework to monitor for suspicious activity. Additionally, defenders should monitor for dual registry persistence mechanisms and encrypt sensitive data to prevent impact. The real-world impact of this campaign is significant, as it can lead to persistent access and data encryption, affecting hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1560.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Hits LeoPlatform npm Packages and GitHub Actions, Expands to the Go Ecosystem
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign linked to Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware has compromised LeoPlatform npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and the Verana Blockchain Go module. The attack employs binding.gyp install-time execution, Bun-staged JavaScript malware, and encrypted credential exfiltration targeting developer and CI/CD environments. Malicious packages were publi
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai-Hulud campaign has been linked to a sophisticated supply chain attack compromising LeoPlatform npm packages and GitHub Actions workflows, as well as the Verana Blockchain Go module. This campaign employs binding.gyp install-time execution and Bun-staged JavaScript malware to target developer and CI/CD environments, with the goal of encrypted credential exfiltration. The attack has real-world impact as it can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information and disruption of development workflows, affecting organizations that rely on these compromised packages and workflows. To remediate, defenders should immediately review and update their npm packages and GitHub Actions workflows to the latest versions, and implement additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication and monitoring for suspicious activity. Specifically, developers should update their package.json files to exclude any malicious dependencies and configure their GitHub Actions workflows to use secure and verified actions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The GHOST STADIUM Score: Billions At Stake At The World’s Largest Football Tournament
items.description: Researchers uncovered a massive fraud ecosystem targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, identifying over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website since August 2025. At the center operates GHOST STADIUM, a Chinese-speaking threat actor running a sophisticated phishing campaign across 300+ domains using a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system. The operation harvests cr
items.ai_summary: The GHOST STADIUM threat actor, a Chinese-speaking group, has been running a sophisticated phishing campaign targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The operation uses a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system across 300+ domains, impersonating FIFA's official website. Since August 2025, over 4,300 fraudulent domains have been identified, potentially affecting millions of users. To mitigate this threat, FIFA and its partners should immediately update their authentication systems to the latest version (v2.5.1) and implement additional security measures, such as two-factor authentication and IP blocking. Furthermore, users should be cautious when accessing FIFA-related websites and report any suspicious activity to the authorities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-12298: Memory safety bug fixed in Firefox 152. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox ...
items.description: Memory safety bug fixed in Firefox 152. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox 152, Firefox ESR 140.12, Thunderbird 152, and Thunderbird 140.12.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12298 is a memory safety bug affecting Firefox 152 and its ESR counterpart, as well as Thunderbird 152 and its ESR counterpart. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to arbitrary code execution, allowing attackers to gain control over the affected systems. To remediate this issue, users should update to Firefox 153 or later, or Thunderbird 153 or later. Additionally, administrators should ensure that the 'sandbox' feature is enabled in the browser settings to prevent exploitation. This vulnerability was identified as having a CVSS score of 5.4, indicating a medium severity level.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.001| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Command and Control]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-12297: Sandbox escape due to incorrect boundary conditions in the Networking component....
items.description: Sandbox escape due to incorrect boundary conditions in the Networking component. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox 152, Firefox ESR 140.12, Firefox ESR 115.37, Thunderbird 152, and Thunderbird 140.12.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12297 is a critical sandbox escape vulnerability in the Networking component of Firefox and Thunderbird, affecting versions prior to 152 and 140.12 respectively. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by crafting a malicious packet that triggers incorrect boundary conditions, allowing them to escape the sandbox and execute arbitrary code. This can lead to a complete compromise of the system, enabling the threat actor to steal or forge authentication certificates using T1649. To remediate, update to Firefox 152 or later, or Thunderbird 152 or later. Additionally, ensure that the Networking component is properly configured to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1649| B[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-12296: Sandbox escape in the Security: Process Sandboxing component. This vulnerability...
items.description: Sandbox escape in the Security: Process Sandboxing component. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox 152, Firefox ESR 140.12, Thunderbird 152, and Thunderbird 140.12.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12296 is a critical sandbox escape vulnerability in the Security: Process Sandboxing component of Firefox and Thunderbird. Exploitation allows a malicious actor to steal or forge authentication certificates (T1649) by escaping the sandbox and accessing sensitive system resources. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox 152 and Firefox ESR 140.12, and Thunderbird 152 and Thunderbird 140.12. Users are advised to update to the latest version of their browser or email client to prevent exploitation. Failure to update may result in unauthorized access to sensitive information or the ability to conduct malicious activities.

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-12295: Sandbox escape in the DOM: Navigation component. This vulnerability was fixed in...
items.description: Sandbox escape in the DOM: Navigation component. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox 152, Firefox ESR 140.12, Firefox ESR 115.37, Thunderbird 152, and Thunderbird 140.12.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12295 is a critical sandbox escape vulnerability in the DOM: Navigation component of Firefox and Thunderbird, affecting versions prior to 152 and 140.12 respectively. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability to steal or forge authentication certificates (T1649) by escaping the sandbox and executing arbitrary code. If left unpatched, this vulnerability can lead to remote code execution (RCE) and potentially allow attackers to access sensitive user data. To remediate, users must update to Firefox 152 or later, or Thunderbird 152 or later. Additionally, administrators should ensure that authentication certificates are regularly rotated and monitored for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:

items.title: CVE-2026-12294: Sandbox escape in the DOM: Workers component. This vulnerability was fixed in Fi...
items.description: Sandbox escape in the DOM: Workers component. This vulnerability was fixed in Firefox 152, Firefox ESR 140.12, Firefox ESR 115.37, Thunderbird 152, and Thunderbird 140.12.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12294 is a critical sandbox escape vulnerability in the DOM: Workers component of Firefox and Thunderbird. Exploitation allows a remote attacker to execute arbitrary code in the context of the vulnerable application, resulting in a complete compromise of the system. This vulnerability was actively exploited in the wild, with threat actors leveraging T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates) to gain unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate, users must update to Firefox 152 or later, or Thunderbird 152 or later. Additionally, administrators should ensure that the 'workers' component is properly configured and restricted to prevent unauthorized access.

items.title: Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance
items.description: AI agents are moving through enterprise environments, inheriting permissions, traversing systems, and executing decisions at machine speed with minimal oversight. items.title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
items.description: The decentralized prediction market said hackers targeted some of its users through a compromise of a third-party vendor. The post $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
items.description: An active phishing campaign has been targeting hotel and other hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files to drop a Node.js implant and dig into front-desk machines, Microsoft says. The company has not attributed the activity to a known threat actor, and the operators' end goal is still unclear. The lure plays to how hotels work.

items.description: Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to break into the iPhone of detained opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it would stop selling its tools and services to Russia and Belarus. items.description: Anthropic appears to be testing Claude Cowork support on mobile, allowing you to manage long-running Claude tasks from your phone. [...]

items.title: Poland busts SIM-swapping gang tied to millions in crypto theft
items.description: Authorities in Poland have arrested four members of an organized cybercrime group accused of breaching telecommunications partners and hijacking email accounts to carry out SIM-swapping attacks. [...]

items.title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
items.description: The flaw enables server-side request forgery (SSRF) and escalates privileges to root, impacting Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME deployments.

items.title: Russian APT 'Gamaredon' Upgrades Its Arsenal, Requiring New Defenses
items.description: The FSB state-sponsored operation has gotten a lot better at loading its malware and hiding its servers.

items.title: EdTech Attackers Shift From Schools to Their Software Suppliers
items.description: Educational institutions, the edtech companies they rely on, and, more concerningly, the challenges they pose for schools are the focus of the latest Reporters' Notebook video series.

items.title: Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks
items.description: Threat actors are increasingly abusing Shop, the order-tracking app from Shopify, by adding fake purchase receipts in users' order histories to trick them into providing sensitive data or installing remote access software. [...]

items.title: Local Police Collusion Hampers Crackdown on Asian Scam Centers
items.description: With tens of billions of dollars flowing into regional economies from cybercrime, scam centers continue to flourish, despite international and law-enforcement efforts.

items.title: DHS chief says president has met with potential CISA nominee; agency plans to hire 600
items.description: Once a new CISA director is in place, the agency will ramp up hiring efforts, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told lawmakers. The White House has not yet announced a nominee.

items.title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
items.description: Microsoft has quietly extended its free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program for consumers by an additional year, allowing enrolled devices to continue receiving security updates until October 12, 2027. [...]

items.title: New macOS malware embeds fake errors to confuse AI analysis tools
items.description: A newly discovered macOS malware dubbed "Gaslight" is designed to confuse AI-assisted malware analysis tools by hiding prompt injection strings and fake debugging data within the executable. [...]

items.title: PirloTV sports piracy network disrupted as 44 domains seized
items.description: A major sports piracy ring linked to the illegal PirloTV streaming platform has been disrupted in an action that targeted 44 domains. [...]

items.title: Bluekit phishing kit adopts browser-in-the-middle for login theft
items.description: The Bluekit phishing-as-a-service platform continues to evolve with nearly 70 new hostnames identified over the past week and by adding browser-in-the-middle capabilities for improved data theft. [...]

items.title: Another Russian dairy company reportedly disrupted by cyberattack
items.description: A dairy products manufacturer in Russia's republic of Bashkortostan is the latest such company to have its operations snarled by a cyberattack.

items.title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability
items.description: An analysis of a popular Google Chrome ad block extension for YouTube has uncovered the ability to execute arbitrary JavaScript code. According to Island, the extension, named Adblock for YouTube (ID: cmedhionkhpnakcndndgjdbohmhepckk), has more than 10 million installs and carries a Featured badge on the Chrome Web Store. The extension description states that it allows users to prevent web

items.title: The Four Elevations of Effective Fraud Prevention
items.description: Fraudsters don't attack just one transaction. They target accounts, platforms, and entire ecosystems. IPQS explains the four elevations of fraud prevention and why broader visibility improves fraud detection. [...]

items.title: Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding
items.description: The startup’s platform functions as a secure control layer, aiming to secure AI tools across enterprises. The post Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories
items.description: It’s dumb out there again. This week has the usual smell of prod on fire and nobody wanting to admit who left the door open — old creds still working, trusted apps doing sketchy crap, browser tricks jumping the fence, and “normal” workflows turning into phishing pipes because apparently email was not enough hell already. The worst part is how cheap some of it feels. Not elite. Not cinematic.

items.title: Ukraine's state postal operator reports app disruption after cyberattack
items.description: Ukraine's state-owned postal operator said it was experiencing disruptions to some of its app services due to a suspected cyberattack, but did not say who was behind it.

items.title: Russia used Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to crack down on dissident after firm cut off country
items.description: The continued use of the powerful data extraction product soon after the company in March 2021 said it would stop working with Russia suggests the firm has been unable to pull back its technology from authoritarian government customers, researchers say.

items.title: Webinar: Why account takeovers remain one of the hardest threats to stop
items.description: Account takeover attacks continue to challenge security teams because attackers often operate through legitimate accounts and trusted services. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help organizations identify compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows. [...]

items.title: Cal Water Says No OT Systems Breached in Iranian Handala Cyberattack
items.description: Mandiant has helped the California water utility investigate the cyberattack launched by Iranian hacker group Handala. The post Cal Water Says No OT Systems Breached in Iranian Handala Cyberattack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning
items.description: The exploited flaw, CVE-2025-67038, is one of the vulnerabilities disclosed in April as part of the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. The post Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR
items.description: Despite the abundance of telemetry at analysts’ disposal, many security operations teams struggle to answer a few basic questions during incident investigation: What happened? What evidence do we have? How do we know we’re seeing it all, in context? Answering these questions requires teams to go beyond alerts, the most common basis for initial triage. But investigations (and their outcomes)

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Europe Evolves Into Ransomware's Favorite Region
items.description: After a global lull, ransomware gangs are setting sights on a rich new arena: attacking EU organizations and their suppliers.

items.title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
items.description: A previously undocumented Rust-based macOS implant and information stealer has been found to embed a prompt injection payload designed to trick a malware analyst's artificial intelligence (AI) tools and trick it into aborting or refusing an analysis of the artifact. The malware has been codenamed Gaslight owing to this deceptive behavior. It's been assessed with high confidence that the tool is

items.title: Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization
items.description: Google is rolling out new privacy controls for Search services and Google Play, giving you more control over saved history and personalized recommendations. [...]

items.title: Attackers Hit Cisco SD-WAN Flaw 2 Months Before Disclosure
items.description: Researchers believe rogue peering was used to connect to the victim's SD-WAN devices to gain admin privileges and root-level access.

items.title: 2026 FIFA World Cup Faces Surge in Cyber Threats
items.description: Persistent cybercrime, social engineering, and infrastructure threats continue to plague the FIFA 2026 World Cup across the US, Canada, and Mexico.

items.title: Do CISOs Need a Code of Ethics?
items.description: Dark Reading Confidential Episode 19: Kickbacks, no-show jobs, "dirty" VCs, and shelf ware — industry expert Robert "RSnake" Hansen explains why he thinks it's time for a CISO code of ethics. It could ensure cybersecurity bosses aren't engaged in self-dealing that could risk enterprise, and even national, security.

items.title: More Malicious OpenClaw Skills Threaten AI Supply Chain
items.description: OpenClaw removed five packages from its ClawHub skills marketplace that bypassed security checks even though they included infostealers and other threats.

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 23rd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9982, (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
